title: Aggregate demand & aggregate supply
summary:
## The big idea Microeconomics asked what sets the price of *one* good. Macroeconomics asks what sets the price level and output of the *whole economy* — and it answers with the same picture, scaled up. **Aggregate demand (AD)** is total planned spending on a country's output; **aggregate supply (AS)** is total planned production. Where they cross, the economy settles at its **equilibrium price level and real GDP**. Every macro topic you will ever study — growth, inflation, unemployment, every policy — is analysed by shifting these two curves. This is the master diagram of the course. ## Aggregate demand: AD = C + I + G + (X − M) Total spending on domestic output has four components: - **C — consumption:** household spending; the biggest slice in almost every economy. Driven by income, interest rates, confidence, wealth (house/share prices). - **I — investment:** firms' spending on capital goods (machines, factories). Driven by interest rates, profit expectations, confidence — the most volatile component. - **G — government spending** on goods and services (schools, roads — *not* transfer payments, which just move income around). - **(X − M) — net exports:** foreigners' spending on our output minus our spending on theirs. Driven by the exchange rate, world incomes and competitiveness. **Why does AD slope down?** A *lower* price level (1) raises the real value of money holdings — people feel wealthier and spend (the **real balance/wealth effect**); (2) tends to lower interest rates, boosting C and I; and (3) makes home goods cheaper than foreign goods (the **trade effect**). So more real output is bought at lower price levels. **AD shifts** whenever a component changes at any given price level: an interest-rate cut, a tax cut, a confidence boom, a weaker currency, faster growth abroad — all shift AD **right**. ## Aggregate supply - **SRAS (short-run)** slopes **upward**: with wages and many costs sticky, a higher price level makes extra output more profitable. SRAS shifts with **production costs**: wages, raw materials and energy, imported input prices (the exchange rate!), indirect taxes. - **LRAS (long-run)** is the economy's **productive capacity** — how much it can produce with all resources fully employed. Its *shape* is one of the great debates: the **classical** view draws it **vertical** (output is fixed by factors and technology; more AD only raises prices), while the **Keynesian** view draws it as a **reverse-L** — horizontal while there is spare capacity, turning vertical only at full employment. LRAS shifts right with more or better labour, capital, technology and productivity — that is **potential growth**, the same thing as the PPC moving outward.
**What each part means:** the axes are now the whole economy's — **price level** (vertical) and **real GDP** (horizontal). The economy starts where **AD1** crosses **AS**: price level **P1**, output **Y1**. Something boosts spending — say an interest-rate cut — and AD shifts right to the dashed **AD2**. The new equilibrium (orange dot) has **higher output Y2** *and* a **higher price level P2**. How the gain splits between extra output and extra inflation depends on how close the economy is to full capacity — the steeper the AS curve at that point, the more you get inflation instead of output.
## The circular flow (the plumbing behind AD) Income circulates between households and firms. **Injections** — investment, government spending, exports (**I + G + X**) — add spending to the flow; **withdrawals/leakages** — saving, taxation, imports (**S + T + M**) — drain it. When injections exceed withdrawals, the flow (national income) expands; equilibrium is where they are equal. (The GDP chapter draws this in full.) ## Using the model — the three headline cases 1. **AD rises with spare capacity** (Keynesian flat zone) → output and jobs rise strongly, little inflation. 2. **AD rises near full capacity** → mostly **demand-pull inflation**, little extra output. 3. **Costs rise** (oil shock, wage surge, weaker currency) → **SRAS shifts left** → prices *up* and output *down* at the same time — **cost-push inflation**, and in its severe form **stagflation** (the 1970s oil crises; 2022's energy shock). ## Why it matters Diagnose-then-prescribe is the exam's favourite macro game: decide *which* curve moved, *which way*, and *how close to capacity* the economy is — and the analysis (and the right policy in Unit 5) follows automatically.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **AD = C + I + G + (X − M)** — know each component's drivers; slopes down via real-balance, interest-rate and trade effects. - **SRAS**: upward; shifts with **costs** (wages, energy, imports, indirect taxes). - **LRAS**: capacity. **Classical = vertical**; **Keynesian = reverse-L** (flat with spare capacity). - LRAS right-shift = **potential growth** = outward PPC. - AD↑ with spare capacity → output↑, little inflation; AD↑ near capacity → **demand-pull inflation**. - SRAS←: **cost-push inflation** + falling output (**stagflation**). - Injections **I+G+X** vs withdrawals **S+T+M** drive the circular flow.

keyTerms.definition: Total planned spending on domestic output: C + I + G + (X − M).
Household spending on goods and services; the largest AD component.
Exports minus imports; a component of AD.
Upward-sloping AS that shifts with production costs.
The economy's productive capacity; vertical (classical) or reverse-L (Keynesian).
Where AD equals AS, setting the price level and real output.
Injections (I, G, X) add to the circular flow; withdrawals (S, T, M) remove from it.

diagrams: **AD/AS equilibrium:** axes = Price Level (vertical) and Real GDP/output (horizontal); downward AD, upward SRAS; mark equilibrium price level and output.
**AD shift:** show AD1 → AD2 and the effect on price level and output (near vs far from capacity).
**SRAS shift:** show a cost rise shifting SRAS left → higher price level, lower output (cost-push/stagflation).
**LRAS shapes:** draw the vertical Classical LRAS and the reverse-L Keynesian LRAS; show LRAS shifting right for potential growth.
tips: Memorise **AD = C + I + G + (X − M)** and be ready to explain how a shock changes a specific component.
Be explicit about **capacity**: the inflation/output effect of an AD rise depends on how close output is to full employment.
Distinguish **SRAS shifts (costs)** from **LRAS shifts (capacity/potential growth)** — a common confusion.
Know both **LRAS shapes** (Keynesian vs Classical) and mention the debate for evaluation marks.
Use the model to define **demand-pull** vs **cost-push** inflation with the correct curve shift.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Clear on AD components and LRAS debate.

revisionNotes:
# AD & AS — quick revision

**Aggregate demand**
- **AD = C + I + G + (X − M)**. C: income, interest rates, confidence, wealth. I: interest rates, expected profit. G: policy. X−M: exchange rate, world growth.
- Downward slope: real-balance effect, interest-rate effect, trade effect.
- AD shifts right: rate cuts, tax cuts, confidence, weak currency, world boom.

**Aggregate supply**
- **SRAS** up-sloping; shifts with **costs**: wages, energy/raw materials, import prices, indirect taxes.
- **LRAS** = capacity: **classical vertical** vs **Keynesian reverse-L** (spare capacity → flat).
- LRAS → right = potential growth (labour, capital, technology, productivity).

**Equilibrium cases**
- AD↑ + spare capacity → big output gain, small price rise.
- AD↑ + near full employment → **demand-pull inflation**.
- SRAS← → **cost-push inflation** + lower output (stagflation).

**Circular flow**
- Injections **I + G + X** vs withdrawals **S + T + M**; equal at equilibrium.

**Exam habits**
- Label axes *Price level* and *Real GDP* (not P and Q).
- Say which curve shifts, why, and how close output is to capacity.
- Mention both LRAS views for evaluation.
questions:
# Practice questions — Aggregate demand & aggregate supply **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Which of the following is **not** a component of aggregate demand? - A — a firm's purchase of new machinery - B — state pension payments to retired workers - C — government spending on new hospitals - D — spending by foreign tourists on hotel stays
▸ Show answer

B. Pensions are transfer payments — income is moved, nothing is produced in return, so they are excluded from G. A is investment, C is government spending, D counts in exports of services.

**2.** The AD curve slopes downward partly because a lower price level: - A — raises the real value of households' money holdings - B — shifts the AD curve to the right - C — raises firms' costs of production - D — causes the government to cut taxes
▸ Show answer

A. The real balance (wealth) effect: cheaper prices make money balances go further, encouraging spending — a movement along AD, not a shift (B confuses the two).

**3.** A sharp rise in world energy prices will, in the importing country: - A — shift AD to the right - B — shift SRAS to the right - C — shift SRAS to the left - D — shift LRAS to the right
▸ Show answer

C. Energy is a cost of production for nearly every firm: costs up → SRAS left → higher price level, lower output (cost-push pressure).

**4.** In the Keynesian model, an increase in AD when the economy has substantial spare capacity causes: - A — a large rise in the price level and no change in output - B — a large rise in output with little change in the price level - C — a fall in output - D — a leftward shift of LRAS
▸ Show answer

B. On the horizontal section of the Keynesian AS curve, idle workers and machines can be hired without bidding up prices, so extra demand mostly becomes extra output.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A developing economy has unemployment of 9% and factories running well below capacity. The government announces a large road- and port-building programme financed by borrowing. Meanwhile, the world price of oil — which the country imports — rises by 30%. The central bank warns about inflation; the finance ministry replies that 'with this much slack, extra spending creates jobs, not price rises'. **(a)** Define 'aggregate demand'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of an AD/AS diagram, the likely effect of the infrastructure programme on output and the price level. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse the effect of the higher oil price on the economy's output and price level. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the finance ministry is right that the extra spending will 'create jobs, not price rises'. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Total planned spending on an economy's output at each price level: C + I + G + (X − M). [1+1]

(b) G rises → AD shifts right; with spare capacity (flat-ish AS), output rises strongly, price level rises little. Correct diagram + application to the data. [4]

(c) Imported oil is a production cost → SRAS shifts left → price level up, output down (cost-push). Note both effects are happening at once: AD right and SRAS left → inflation likely; output effect ambiguous. [6]

(d) For the ministry: 9% unemployment and idle factories = Keynesian flat zone; multiplier creates jobs. Against: the oil shock is already pushing prices up; bottlenecks can appear before 'full' employment; borrowing may raise interest rates (crowding out); classical view predicts price rises. Judgement: probably more jobs than inflation while slack lasts, but the claim overstates certainty. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the components of aggregate demand and why the AD curve slopes downward. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether an increase in aggregate demand always benefits an economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) C, I, G, X−M with drivers; slope via real-balance, interest-rate and trade effects (any two well explained).

(b) Benefits: higher output, employment, incomes — especially with spare capacity; helps hit growth targets. Costs: demand-pull inflation near capacity; worsened current account (imports rise); may be unsustainable (credit booms). Depends on the output gap and what caused the rise. Reasoned judgement required.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the factors that shift the short-run and the long-run aggregate supply curves. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether the Keynesian or the classical view of aggregate supply gives better guidance to policymakers in a recession. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) SRAS: cost factors (wages, energy, import prices, indirect taxes). LRAS: quantity/quality of factors, technology, productivity — potential growth (link to PPC).

(b) Keynesian: spare capacity → demand stimulus raises output not prices → supports intervention (2008/2020 responses). Classical: output returns to the full-employment level; stimulus only causes inflation → prefer supply-side reform. Evaluate with evidence and time horizons (short-run Keynesian, long-run classical); a justified conclusion.
